questions.stem: A 28-year-old Turkish man presents with a 3-month history of recurrent, painful oral ulcers that heal leaving scars. He also reports painful genital ulcers and recent onset of red, painful eyes with blurred vision. On examination, there are aphthous ulcers in the mouth, a scrotal ulcer, and conjunctival injection with hypopyon on slit-lamp examination. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of recurrent oral ulcers (with scarring), genital ulcers, and uveitis with hypopyon is classic for Behçet's disease. Recurrent aphthous stomatitis lacks genital and ocular involvement. Crohn's disease can cause oral ulcers but typically has gastrointestinal symptoms. Reactive arthritis follows an infection and involves urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis. Herpes simplex causes vesicular lesions not aphthous ulcers.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with recurrent oral ulcers for 2 years and intermittent arthralgia affecting her knees and ankles. She also notices tender, red, raised lesions on her shins that resolve with bruising (erythema nodosum). She has no genital ulcers, eye symptoms, or gastrointestinal complaints. Blood tests show mild anaemia and elevated ESR. A pathergy test is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Erythema nodosum associated with streptococcal infection
questions.explanation: The combination of recurrent oral ulcers, arthralgia, erythema nodosum, and a positive pathergy test is highly suggestive of Behçet's disease. Sarcoidosis often has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and pulmonary involvement. Crohn's disease typically presents with abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Lupus would show malar rash, photosensitivity, and systemic features. Isolated erythema nodosum from streptococcal infection would not have chronic oral ulcers.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known Behçet's disease (on colchicine and azathioprine) presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe headache, fever (38.5°C), neck stiffness, and photophobia. He is drowsy but rousable. CSF analysis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (200 cells/µL), elevated protein, and normal glucose. Gram stain and culture are negative. MRI brain shows a small area of T2 hyperintensity in the brainstem. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presentation of meningoencephalitis with lymphocytic CSF, negative cultures, and brainstem involvement in a patient with known Behçet's disease is most consistent with neuro-Behçet. Bacterial meningitis would have neutrophilic CSF and positive Gram stain. Viral meningitis is possible but less likely with focal brainstem MRI changes. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis typically presents with headache and papilloedema, not fever and meningism. Migraine does not cause fever or CSF pleocytosis.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of recurrent oral and genital ulcers presents to the ophthalmology clinic with acute onset of blurred vision, floaters, and redness in her left eye. On examination, visual acuity is reduced (6/18), there is ciliary injection, and anterior chamber cells are seen. Fundoscopy reveals vitreous haze and retinal vasculitis. She has no other new symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of anterior uveitis and retinal vasculitis with vitreous haze in a patient with known recurrent oral/genital ulcers is classic for Behçet's disease. Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with severe pain, hard eye, and fixed mid-dilated pupil. Cataract is painless with gradual blurring. Retinal vein occlusion causes sudden painless vision loss with retinal haemorrhages. Vitreous haemorrhage is usually due to diabetes or trauma.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) presents with a painful genital ulcer of 3 days' duration. She has a history of recurrent painful mouth ulcers and one episode of genital ulcers 2 years ago that resolved spontaneously. She has no fever or lymphadenopathy. On examination, there is a single, deep, well-demarcated ulcer on the labium minus with a clean base, not vesicular. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of recurrent oral and genital ulcers, with the current genital ulcer being deep, clean-based, and painless (though Behçet's ulcers can be painful), is most consistent with Behçet's disease, which can flare in pregnancy. Herpes genitalis presents with grouped vesicular lesions that rupture into painful erosions. Primary syphilis chancre is usually solitary, painless, but associated with lymphadenopathy. Chancroid is painful and purulent. Crohn's genital involvement typically occurs with active gastrointestinal disease.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man of Middle Eastern origin presents with recurrent painful oral ulcers (more than 3 episodes in 12 months) and two episodes of genital ulcers. He has no other symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm a diagnosis of Behçet disease?
questions.explanation: The International Study Group criteria require recurrent oral ulcers plus at least two of: genital ulcers, eye lesions, skin lesions, or positive pathergy test. This patient has only one additional feature (genital ulcers); a pathergy test can provide the second criterion. HLA-B51 is supportive but not diagnostic; colonoscopy is for IBD; slit-lamp and skin biopsy are targeted tests, not the next step for confirming diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with known Behçet disease (diagnosed 2 years ago with oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and arthritis) now presents with bilateral red eyes, photophobia, and blurred vision. What is the most appropriate initial investigation to guide diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Slit-lamp examination is the gold standard to detect anterior uveitis or hypopyon, which are common ocular manifestations of Behçet disease. Visual acuity tests vision loss but not the cause; fundoscopy examines the posterior segment; fluorescein angiography and OCT are secondary tests.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known Behçet disease (oral ulcers, pathergy positive, erythema nodosum) presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe headache, vomiting, and right arm weakness. CT head is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to identify the cause?
questions.explanation: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is a known neurological complication of Behçet disease, and MR venogram is the non-invasive investigation of choice to confirm it. MRI brain with contrast may show parenchymal lesions but is less sensitive for sinus thrombosis; CT venogram exposes to radiation; lumbar puncture is needed only if meningitis is suspected; carotid Doppler is for arterial disease.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with known Behçet disease (oral and genital ulcers, uveitis) develops a positive pathergy test during a routine clinic visit. What does a positive pathergy test indicate?
questions.explanation: A positive pathergy test indicates skin hyperreactivity to needle prick, which is a characteristic feature of Behçet disease. It does not correlate with disease activity, prognosis, or the need for treatment; it is one of several diagnostic criteria, not confirmatory alone.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old pregnant woman (16 weeks gestation) with a history of recurrent oral and genital ulcers suspects a flare of her Behçet disease. She has developed three painful oral ulcers in the past week. Which of the following is the most appropriate investigation to assess disease activity in this setting?
questions.explanation: Ocular involvement in Behçet disease can be asymptomatic and sight-threatening; slit-lamp examination is essential to detect uveitis even without eye symptoms. Pathergy test is not for assessing activity; ESR/CRP are nonspecific and elevated in pregnancy; skin biopsy and HLA-B51 are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with recurrent painful oral ulcers and painful red nodules on her shins (erythema nodosum) over the past 4 months. She also has two episodes of genital ulceration. No eye or neurological symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For mild-to-moderate mucocutaneous Behçet disease without major organ involvement, colchicine is first-line therapy (NICE/BNF). Topical corticosteroids may be used for oral ulcers but not as systemic first-line.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man with known Behçet disease develops acute onset of blurred vision, eye pain, and photophobia in his right eye. Slit-lamp examination reveals anterior and posterior uveitis with retinal vasculitis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Acute severe panuveitis in Behçet requires high-dose systemic corticosteroids (oral or IV) as first-line to rapidly control inflammation; topical therapy is insufficient for posterior involvement, and immunosuppressants are steroid-sparing agents.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with Behçet disease presents with headache, diplopia, and left-sided weakness. MRI brain shows a brainstem lesion consistent with parenchymal neuro-Behçet. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days
questions.explanation: Acute parenchymal CNS involvement in Behçet is treated with high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) as first-line, followed by oral taper; other immunosuppressants are used for maintenance or refractory disease.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with Behçet disease presents with haemoptysis and chest pain. CT pulmonary angiogram shows a right lower lobe pulmonary artery aneurysm. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary artery aneurysm in Behçet is a severe vascular complication requiring aggressive immunosuppression with cyclophosphamide and high-dose corticosteroids as first-line; anticoagulation is controversial and not first-line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with Behçet disease presents with recurrent oral ulcers. She is 12 weeks pregnant. Which of the following therapies is contraindicated in her pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. Colchicine and prednisolone are relatively safe; azathioprine may be used with caution; topical corticosteroids are safe.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with recurrent painful oral ulcers for 6 months and two episodes of genital ulcers. She has no eye symptoms or arthritis. Examination shows multiple aphthous ulcers on buccal mucosa and a healing ulcer on the labia. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Suspected Behçet disease requires specialist input for confirmation and management. Topical lidocaine provides symptomatic relief but does not address the underlying diagnosis. A pathergy test is rarely performed and not immediately indicated. Colchicine or steroids might be used after rheumatology assessment.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with known Behçet disease (oral/genital ulcers, arthritis) presents with new tender red nodules on his shins (erythema nodosum) and bilateral knee arthralgia. He is currently taking colchicine 500 mcg twice daily. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: For a moderate mucocutaneous and articular flare despite maximum colchicine, a short course of oral prednisolone is first-line. Azathioprine is reserved for severe or refractory disease. NSAIDs can be added for arthritis but are not the single most important action. Dermatology referral is unnecessary for this typical lesion.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known Behçet disease (history of uveitis) presents with acute onset severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. His temperature is 38.2°C, CRP 80 mg/L, WCC 12 x10^9/L. CT head is normal. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Acute neurological symptoms with meningism require CSF analysis to differentiate meningitis from CNS Behçet. High-dose steroids are given after excluding infection. Antibiotics or antivirals should not be started empirically before CSF results. Cyclophosphamide is not first-line for acute neurological involvement.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with Behçet disease presents with sudden onset of pleuritic chest pain and haemoptysis. CT pulmonary angiography shows a pulmonary artery aneurysm. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary artery aneurysm is a life-threatening complication requiring immediate high-dose IV steroids to suppress inflammation and prevent rupture. Anticoagulation is contraindicated due to haemoptysis and rupture risk. Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy. Cyclophosphamide is a second-line agent.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman (16 weeks gestation) with Behçet disease in remission on azathioprine 150 mg daily develops a small painful oral ulcer. She has no other symptoms. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Continue azathioprine and add topical triamcinolone
questions.explanation: Azathioprine is relatively safe in pregnancy and should be continued to maintain remission. A mild oral ulcer can be managed with topical steroids. Stopping azathioprine risks a flare. Prednisolone or switching to ciclosporin is unnecessary. Referral may be considered but is not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with a 1-year history of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers presents with acute onset of red, painful left eye and blurred vision. On examination, visual acuity is 6/36 in that eye, and there is ciliary flush and a visible layer of white cells (hypopyon) in the anterior chamber. Which of the following findings in this patient is most indicative of a sight-threatening emergency?
questions.explanation: Hypopyon uveitis is a classic and severe ocular manifestation of Behçet disease that requires urgent ophthalmology referral to prevent permanent vision loss. The other findings are characteristic but not acutely sight-threatening.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known Behçet disease on colchicine for mucocutaneous involvement presents with sudden-onset sharp right-sided chest pain, dyspnoea, and mild haemoptysis. Oxygen saturations are 94% on air. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening complication of Behçet disease in this patient?
questions.explanation: Haemoptysis in a patient with Behçet disease should raise suspicion for a pulmonary artery aneurysm, a life-threatening vasculitic complication that can rupture. Although pulmonary embolism is a differential, the haemoptysis points more to aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with known Behçet disease presents with a 3-day history of severe global headache, nausea, and blurred vision. He has no photophobia or neck stiffness. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. CT head is unremarkable. Which of the following must be urgently ruled out?
questions.explanation: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) is a recognised neurovascular complication of Behçet disease presenting with headache and papilloedema. CT head is often normal, and MR venography is required. Subarachnoid haemorrhage is less likely given the normal CT and absence of neck stiffness.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with Behçet disease presents with sudden onset of severe central abdominal pain, vomiting, and fever. She has not passed stool for 2 days. Examination reveals abdominal rigidity and guarding. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Gastrointestinal involvement in Behçet disease can cause deep, punched-out ulcers that may perforate, leading to an acute abdomen. A history of Behçet makes intestinal ulcer perforation the most likely cause.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with Behçet disease who is currently in remission on azathioprine and low-dose prednisolone presents to the obstetric clinic at 20 weeks gestation. She has no current symptoms. She has a history of recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and one episode of anterior uveitis. Which of the following pre-existing complications would most significantly increase the risk of a life-threatening event during pregnancy or delivery?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary artery aneurysm is a life-threatening vascular complication of Behçet disease that can rupture during pregnancy or labour due to haemodynamic changes. This requires high-risk obstetric and multidisciplinary management.
